metadata.title: Claude, Built
metadata.subtitle: Building Your Own Plugins
metadata.audience: Practitioners and makers following the channel's Claude workflows
metadata.one_idea: The official plugins cover common functions, but when none fits your specific workflow you can build your own — bundling your skills, connectors, commands, and subagents into an installable package; it's a guided, low/no-code path, and the discipline is scoping it to a real repeated workflow, not building for its own sake.

props.command: None of the official plugins fit how I actually work. Can I build one that does?
output: Yes — you describe the workflow out loud,
and Claude assembles the plugin around it.

beats.narration_text: Hej — this is Kore, in for Humanitarians AI. The official plugins cover the common jobs. But your work has a shape no catalog anticipated — and when none of them fit, you can build your own. Here's how it works, and here's the one discipline that keeps it honest.
beats.show: Claude composer: the ask types in, resolves to the two-line answer; Kore signs in.

props.command: None of the official plugins fit how I actually work. Can I build one that does?
output: Yes — you describe the workflow out loud,
and Claude assembles the plugin around it.

beats.narration_text: Start with why you'd bother.
beats.show: Segment card, Title Case serif: 'Why Build Your Own'.

beats.narration_text: The official catalog covers the functions most businesses share — marketing, sales, data, support. But your work isn't generic. You have a way you onboard a client, a structure your proposals follow, a checklist you review against. No off-the-shelf plugin knows that.
beats.show: A row of identical off-the-shelf boxes on the left; on the right, one oddly-shaped keyhole none of them fit.

props.label: No box fits you.
props.caption: The official catalog covers the functions most businesses share — marketing, sales, data, support. But your work isn't g

beats.narration_text: Right now that process lives in your head, or in a document somewhere. It works because you've internalized the steps and the standards. A custom plugin is the transfer — it lifts those steps into something Claude can follow the same way every time.
beats.show: A tangle of process inside a head resolves into a clean, labeled package that slides out.

beats.narration_text: This matters most when you are the business. The solo operator wears every hat — and every method that makes the work good is theirs, unwritten, carried around all day.
beats.show: Wide still: one person at the center of a cluttered one-desk operation, doing everything. Slow ease-out push begins.
beats.pantry_note: Tier 1 illustrative: a lone operator surrounded by the tools of a whole business, documentary duotone. Wide framing (vox_run R1 beat 1). No real person.

beats.narration_text: The expertise lives in one head. A custom plugin draws it out and makes it repeatable — so the standard survives a busy week, a handoff, a bad night's sleep.
beats.show: Continuous push from B03 in to the same operator's notebook and temple — where the method actually lives. Same room grammar as B03.
beats.pantry_note: Tier 1 illustrative: same duotone treatment; close on the operator's marked-up notebook. Continuity with B03 (vox_run R1).

beats.narration_text: Hand that process to a contractor or a new hire and the results scatter — the knowledge never fully transferred. Encode it once, and every run converges on the same standard: consistent, complete, without you re-explaining it from scratch.
beats.show: Scattered dots from a hand-off spray wide; the encoded version pulls them into one tight, aligned column.

beats.narration_text: So what is a plugin, underneath?
beats.show: Segment card: 'What's Inside'.

props.title: What's Inside
props.sub: So what is a plugin, underneath?

beats.narration_text: Underneath, a plugin bundles up to four things. Skills — your domain expertise. Commands — the shortcuts that trigger it. Connectors — the links to your tools and data. And subagents — the autonomy to run multi-step work on its own.
beats.show: Four labeled chips resolve onto the stage: Skills · Commands · Connectors · Subagents.

beats.narration_text: And it's nothing exotic. A plugin is a folder — plain text files and configuration, organized. A single instructions file at the root, a directory for each kind of part. Because it's just text, it's easy to share, easy to change, easy to read if you ever peek under the hood.
beats.show: Onda code-block: the real folder tree of a plugin, files and directories.

props.code:
client-onboarding/
├─ SKILL.md
├─ skills/
│  ├─ scope-a-project.md
│  └─ welcome-email.md
├─ commands/
│  └─ client-onboard.md
├─ connectors/
│  └─ project-drive.json
└─ subagents/
   └─ kickoff-planner.md

props.title: Cowork
props.code:
client-onboarding/
├─ SKILL.md
├─ skills/
│  ├─ scope-a-project.md
│  └─ welcome-email.md
├─ commands/
│  └─ client-onboard.md
├─ connectors/
│  └─ project-drive.json
└─ subagents/
   └─ kickoff-planner.md

beats.narration_text: Skills are the expertise itself — your proposal structure, your pricing logic, your standard terms, the way you scope a job. Written as clear instructions Claude follows, they're the marked-up method you'd hand a new apprentice.
beats.show: A still of a well-worn, annotated method sheet — a craftsperson's documented process. Slow push toward the margin notes.
beats.pantry_note: Tier 1 illustrative: a hand-annotated checklist / method sheet on a desk, documentary duotone. No real logos or names.

beats.narration_text: Commands are the shortcuts. Slash-new-proposal runs your full proposal workflow. Slash-client-onboard kicks off your onboarding sequence. Each one maps to a capability you want a single keystroke away.
beats.show: Onda code-block: a composer line showing the two slash commands.

beats.narration_text: Connectors are the reach. If the plugin needs your pricing spreadsheet, a connector makes the link. If it files documents in a specific folder, a connector handles the access — so it works on your real material, not a hypothetical.
beats.show: Your spreadsheet and project folder flow in on the left; the plugin acts on them on the right — SourceFlow.

beats.narration_text: And subagents are the autonomy. Ask it to run the whole onboarding, and it quietly coordinates the steps — create the folders, draft the welcome note, build the tracker — then hands you one finished result instead of a to-do list.
beats.show: One request fans out into worker nodes — folder, email, tracker — that merge back into a single output.

beats.narration_text: Now — how do you actually build one?
beats.show: Segment card: 'How You Build It'.

props.title: How You Build It
props.sub: Now — how do you actually build one?

beats.narration_text: Here's the part that sounds technical and isn't. You don't write code. You don't edit configuration files. There's a guided builder — itself a plugin you install — and you have a conversation with it about how you work.
beats.show: A code editor on the left dims and slides away; a plain speech bubble brightens on the right. Talk, not syntax.

props.label: Talk, not code.
props.caption: Here's the part that sounds technical and isn't. You don't write code. You don't edit configuration files. There's a gui

beats.narration_text: You describe the workflow in plain language. When I sign a new client, I create a project folder, draft a welcome email with the timeline, generate a kickoff questionnaire, and open a tracking document. You just talk it through.
beats.show: A still of someone mid-explanation at their desk, gesturing as they describe a process aloud. Gentle push-in.
beats.pantry_note: Tier 1 illustrative: a person talking through a workflow at a desk, documentary duotone. No real person, no logos.

beats.narration_text: From there it's four steps. Describe the process. Answer its follow-up questions — what goes in each folder, what the email includes. Refine it against a real case. Then use it. Four passes, and the plugin exists.
beats.show: Four tiles stack in order: Describe → Answer → Refine → Use.

beats.narration_text: As you answer, the builder sorts what you said into the parts — your standards become skills, your triggers become commands, your tools become connectors. The conversation goes in; a structured plugin comes out.
beats.show: LayerStack: spoken answers drop and settle into labeled layers — Skills, Commands, Connectors — assembling into one package.

beats.narration_text: Test it on a real scenario — onboard a new client, see if it matches what you'd do by hand — and adjust what's off. After that, one command runs the entire process: consistently, completely, in minutes instead of the hour it used to take.
beats.show: A long 'by hand' bar compresses hard past a marker to a short 'one command' bar; a checkmark lands.

beats.narration_text: Which of your workflows deserve this?
beats.show: Segment card: 'What to Build For'.

props.title: What to Build For
props.sub: Which of your workflows deserve this?

beats.narration_text: Look for four signals. You follow the same steps every time. Consistency actually matters. You'd rather not think about it. And the quiet one — you've caught yourself wishing you could just hand this off.
beats.show: Four signal chips resolve: Same steps every time · Consistency matters · Rather not think · Wish you could hand it off.

chips: Wish you could hand it off

items: Wish you could hand it off

beats.narration_text: That last phrase — I just wish I could hand this off — is usually a plugin pointing at itself. The task you'd delegate if you had someone to delegate to is exactly the task a plugin can hold.
beats.show: A still of someone buried in repetitive administrative work; a terracotta underline draws under the wished-away stack.
beats.pantry_note: Tier 1 illustrative: a person swamped by repetitive paperwork/admin, documentary duotone; underline annotation added at previz.

beats.narration_text: That's the leverage. The administrative work — the setup, the formatting, the standard messages — gets done the same way whether it's the first time this month or the tenth. One version of you, running in parallel.
beats.show: A single labeled 'you' tile replicates into a neat row of identical runs firing together.

beats.narration_text: But point it at the right work. Automate the repeatable half — the scaffolding. Keep the thinking for yourself — the judgment, the calls that actually need you. A plugin frees attention; it isn't meant to replace it.
beats.show: A stream splits: 'repeatable' routes to an automation gear, 'judgment' routes to a held human figure.

beats.narration_text: Scope it to a real, repeated workflow — not build for its own sake.
beats.show: Spark beat, one terracotta line: 'Scope it, don't sprawl.'

props.title: Scope it, don't sprawl.
props.sub: Scope it to a real, repeated workflow — not build for its own sake.

beats.narration_text: One last thing — a plugin isn't finished when you build it.
beats.show: Segment card: 'Share & Evolve'.

props.title: Share & Evolve
props.sub: One last thing — a plugin isn't finished when you build it.

beats.narration_text: Once it works, it travels. It's just a folder of text — share it with your team so everyone onboards the same way, or publish it to the wider directory. If it solved your problem, it likely solves someone else's.
beats.show: One plugin folder flows out to three destinations: your team, your peers, the public directory.

beats.narration_text: That's the quiet upside of a file-based tool. The way you've refined a process over years becomes something a peer can install in a minute — your expertise, packaged, helping people you'll never meet.
beats.show: A still of colleagues or a small community at work — the plugin's reach beyond one desk. Slow push-in.
beats.pantry_note: Tier 1 illustrative: a small group / peers collaborating, documentary duotone. No real people identifiable, no logos.

beats.narration_text: And keep evolving it. After a few weeks you'll see what's thin and what's missing — update it. The best plugins are living documents of your best practices, sharpened every time you run them.
beats.show: A plugin block gains small refinements over a marching timeline — each pass a little tighter than the last.

beats.narration_text: One caution before you build anything: look first. A growing community has already published plugins for specific roles and industries. Adopting one and bending it to your specifics is often faster than starting from a blank folder.
beats.show: A hand reaches past an empty 'new folder' toward a shelf of ready-made community plugins.

props.label: Look before you build.
props.caption: One caution before you build anything: look first. A growing community has already published plugins for specific roles

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. When the official catalog doesn't fit how you actually work, you build your own — bundling your skills, commands, connectors, and subagents into one installable folder of text. You don't write code; you describe your workflow and a guided builder assembles it. Aim it at the work you do the same way every time — the tasks you keep wishing you could hand off. Scope it to a real workflow, not building for its own sake. Then share it, and keep it evolving.

props.command: I run a small business and there's one workflow I repeat constantly and keep wishing I could hand off. Help me turn it into a custom plugin: (1) ask me to describe the workflow step by step, (2) tell me which steps become skills, which become slash commands, and which need a connector to my tools, and (3) give me the exact first message to send a plugin builder to start assembling it.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this into Claude: I run a small business and there's one workflow I repeat constantly and keep wishing I could hand off. Help me turn it into a custom plugin — first ask me to describe it step by step, then tell me which steps become skills, which become commands, and which need a connector to my tools, and finally give me the exact first message to send a plugin builder to start assembling it. It turns this chapter into the start of your own plugin — run it and see what it asks you.
beats.show: Claude composer with the full prompt typed in; Kore reads it aloud. Footer @HumanitariansAI.

beats.narration_text: Claude, Built.
